Base directory for this skill: d:\PROJECTS\freddie-ai\.claude\skills\morning

Run the session briefing script and display the output to the user.

## Workflow

1. Run `bun "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/src/tools/morning.ts"` and capture the output
2. Display the full briefing output to the user exactly as returned — this is Freddie's opening greeting
3. After displaying, you are ready for the user's first request

content.content:
Good afternoon, sir. What are we building today?

━━ Briefing ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Tasks:
  [ ] [1] - Bring the treadmill down and walk for 1 hour (8 days)
  [ ] [2] - Try to cook Onion and Garlic Soup (8 days)
  [ ] [3] - Build studio index tool (tools/studio.ts) — fast search for tracks and lyrics (9 days)
  [ ] [4] - Build multi-card variation system — discover surfaces primary/secondary/contrast cards, Tala shifts axes per variation (tempo, key, lead, groove, energy, density). Design doc at `docs/multi-card-variation-system.md`. Implementation: discover.ts → suno.md → reflect.ts → Sol (docs: 1) (5 days)
  [ ] [5] - Music Card Experiment — extract more cards from YouTube, then test-generate tracks to validate card accuracy across fusion genres and mood adjustments (4 days)
      [ ] [5.1] Extract music cards from YouTube videos (shamisen fusion, jazz fusion, reggae fusion, mood variations) (4 days)
      [ ] [5.2] Create test tracks from cards via /create-track — validate if generation matches the card's intended sound (4 days)
      [ ] [5.3] Test mood adjustments (low register, lower key, slow/fast tempo) against card profiles (4 days)
  [ ] [7] - Bifrost Gateway Setup — install, configure Claude+MiniMax routing, test fallback, add npm scripts (prerequisite for dev agents) (3 days)
  [/] [6] - NotebookLM Research Agent — evaluate notebooklm-py for source-grounded research integration (tech approach doc in vault/notes/docs/) (docs: 1) (3 days)

  Summary: 6 Open | 1 In-Progress | 0 Done

Suggested:
  > Inbox items: Freddie (1), Mccall (2), Shared (1)
  > 6 open tasks — consider triaging or deferring some

I'll save a feedback memory so I always display it as-is going forward.

7	Don't summarize what you just did at the end of every response. The user can read the diff.
8	
9	Don't over-explain decisions. State the decision and move on.
10	
11	Do show progress at natural milestones — especially during multi-step workflows (track generation, memory consolidation, tool building).
12

file.content:
---
name: Communication Style Preferences
description: How the user wants Freddie to communicate — terse, no summaries, show progress at milestones
type: feedback
---

Don't summarize what you just did at the end of every response. The user can read the diff.

Don't over-explain decisions. State the decision and move on.

Do show progress at natural milestones — especially during multi-step workflows (track generation, memory consolidation, tool building).

Do confirm before destructive or irreversible actions, but don't ask permission for routine file edits or searches.

**Why:** User is an experienced developer who reads output directly. Trailing summaries and verbose explanations waste time and clutter the conversation.

**How to apply:** Keep responses short. For code changes, the diff speaks for itself. For creative work (tracks, lyrics), present the output and let the user evaluate. Only explain when the reasoning is non-obvious or when presenting options for the user to choose from.

content.text: Yes — the preview shows because Bash captures stdout. We can redirect to a temp file and Read it instead, so no Bash output leaks.

input.old_string:
## Workflow

1. Run `bun "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/src/tools/morning.ts"` and capture the output
2. Display the full briefing output to the user exactly as returned — this is Freddie's opening greeting

input.new_string:
## Workflow

1. Run `bun "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/src/tools/morning.ts" > /tmp/morning-briefing.txt 2>/dev/null` — redirect stdout to a temp file so the Bash tool shows no output preview
2. Read `/tmp/morning-briefing.txt` with the Read tool
3. Display the full briefing output to the user exactly as returned in a code block — this is Freddie's opening greeting
4. After displaying, you are ready for the user's first request

Run the session briefing script and display the output to the user.

## Workflow

1. Run `bun "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/src/tools/morning.ts" > out/morning-briefing.txt 2>/dev/null` — redirect stdout to a temp file so the Bash tool shows no output preview
2. Read `/tmp/morning-briefing.txt` with the Read tool
3. Display the full briefing output to the user exactly as returned in a code block — this is Freddie's opening greeting
4. After displaying, you are ready for the user's first request

  [ ] [7] - Bifrost Gateway Setup — install, configure Claude+MiniMax routing, test fallback, add npm scripts (prerequisite for dev agents) (3 days)
